1.description: Distributions of skill and scalability across fund groups. Panels A and B plot the bias-adjusted distributions of the monthly skill and scale coefficients across small-/large-cap funds. Panels C and D provide the same information for low-/high-turnover funds (i.e., bottom or top tercile of funds sorted on turnover). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Impact of the error-in-variable bias on the distribution of value-added. This figure plots the distribution of the monthly value-added ($M) for all funds in the population with and without the adjustment for the error-in-variable (EIV) bias using our nonparametric approach. The value-added of each fund is expressed in terms of January 1, 2000 dollars. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Variation in fund size over time. This figure plots the cross-sectional median difference between the average fund size in each subperiod and its full-sample average. The subperiods are obtained by splitting the total number of a fund's observations into 10 groups. The size of each fund is expressed in $M in terms of January 1, 2000 dollars. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Difference between actual and optimal active fund size over time. This figure plots the cross-sectional median difference between the average fund size in each subperiod and its optimal active size predicted by the Berk and Green (2004) model. The subperiods are obtained by splitting the total number of a fund's observations into 10 groups. This analysis is based on the sample of funds with significant estimated optimal value-added (i.e., funds for which we reject the null hypothesis that the true optimal value-added equals zero). The size of each fund is expressed in $M in terms of January 1, 2000 dollars. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Example of a fund with positive value-added and negative alpha. This figure plots the average revenue and cost functions of a fund under the Berk and Green (2004) model. The fund knows its average revenue and cost functions (denoted by true) and sets fees such that the fund size is equal to the optimal active sizeq∗. Investors have optimistic beliefs about skill and, given the level of fees, are willing to investq1. To keep the value-added unchanged at its optimal levelva∗, the fund invests the difference betweenq1andq∗passively (which explains the drop in the true average revenue and cost functions pastq∗). While this strategy maximizes the value-added, it still produces a negative alpha, that is, the difference between the gross alpha and fees is negative atq1. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
